system_description.overview: DekaBridge provides a secure, cloud-based AI platform designed to augment strategic decision-making for enterprise product leaders. It uses proprietary AI agents including simulated stakeholders and a bias-aware Decision Coach to help users analyze proposals, plan roadmaps, and test strategies in a simulated environment.

products.DekaBridge Application: Primary web application (Next.js/React SPA) providing all client-facing features
products.Data Ingestion and Processing: Securely uploads, processes, and indexes client-provided corporate data into a database with logical per-client segregation
products.DekaBridge Agent: Central conversational AI for creating and refining strategic proposals
products.Simulation Room: Environment for users to interact with multiple customizable AI persona agents (CEO, Head of Sales, Chief Legal Counsel, etc.) to test and validate strategies
products.Decision Coach: AI-powered service that monitors conversations for cognitive bias and provides real-time, evidence-based feedback

infrastructure.cloud_provider: AWS (carve-out — AWS controls excluded from scope)
infrastructure.physical_presence: None — no physical offices or servers
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (multiple availability zones referenced for failover)
serverless: AWS App Runner (application servers per network diagram)
infrastructure.cdn: Vercel (hosts www servers and application servers per network diagram)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: AWS VPC with private subnets; ACLs in place; segmented VPCs; production network isolated
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols, and services; firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS via AWS to identify and protect against threats
network_architecture.vpn: Bastion host with VPN for remote access
network_architecture.tls: TLS using certificates from an established third-party certificate authority; HTTPS + auth on App Runner
network_architecture.content_filtering: Content filtering system routes all internet traffic through a proxy server
network_architecture.topology: Internet → Firewall → Vercel (www + application servers) → AWS IAM + TLS → App Runner; AWS VPC in us-east-2 with private subnet containing Amazon RDS (PostgreSQL); S3, AWS Bedrock, and DynamoDB also in AWS infra

application_architecture.pattern: Hybrid (SPA frontend + serverless-style backend on App Runner + managed AI services)
application_architecture.environments: Separate development/staging and production environments; changes tested in dev/stage before production

data_storage.backup_frequency: Backup policy exists; formal backup and recovery procedures defined; retention periods defined

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: AWS IAM; passwordless systems mentioned for user auth
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform; MFA required for source code tool access
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day of termination
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Passwords controlled through AWS password policy; periodic forced changes, expiry, complexity requirements; account lockout after failed attempts

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used to encrypt data at rest
encryption.key_management: AWS KMS (cloud provider key management service)

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Source code management tool (specific name not disclosed — GitHub/GitLab inferred from MFA requirement)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured so every merge request requires additional approval before moving to production
ci_cd_devops.iac: Configuration management tool used for baseline management and rollback capability
ci_cd_devops.container_orchestration: AWS App Runner (managed container-like runtime; not Kubernetes)
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: CTO sets up automatic patch management; monthly patch management reviews; OS-critical patches applied as available; workstations scanned daily for patch compliance

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not explicitly named — monitoring tool generates alerts on predefined thresholds
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled at application and infrastructure level; access to modify logs is restricted
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts to IT management for high and critical risk events; ticketing/change-management application tracks alerts

third_party_services.purpose: Primary cloud infrastructure — compute, storage, database, AI (Bedrock), networking
Frontend hosting / CDN for www and application servers

security_tools.antivirus: Endpoint Security Solution installed on all desktops and laptops; anti-virus and malware protection on all production servers; definitions updated weekly on servers, daily on workstations
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications; scan frequency adjusted as needed; internal IT team performs periodic vulnerability scans
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party

excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — AWS responsible for physical security of data center; not in scope
A1.2 — DekaBridge does not have an office; environmental security managed by building/AWS
Processing Integrity — determined not relevant to the system
notable_controls: CC8.1.2 — Branch protection rules require additional approval for every merge to production
CC8.1.3 — Source code tool restricted to MFA-authenticated users
CC6.2.2 — Quarterly user access reviews for all accounts including privileged users
CC6.1.4 — Access revoked within one business day of termination
CC7.1.1 — Baseline configs retained as code with rollback capability (IaC-style)
CC7.4.1 — Annual BCDR testing across multiple loss scenarios
CC4.1.1 — Annual external penetration test by independent third party

infrastructure.rationale: Single AWS region (us-east-2) with multi-AZ failover. Vercel for frontend CDN. App Runner for compute. No multi-region deployment. IaC-style baseline config management referenced but no named IaC tool (Terraform/CDK).

application_architecture.rationale: Modern SPA (Next.js/React) with App Runner backend and AWS Bedrock for AI. Multi-tenant with logical data segregation. Separate environments with branch protection and code review. Languages and CI/CD tool not named. Microservices vs. monolith architecture not clarified.

data_layer.rationale: Two database types (RDS PostgreSQL + DynamoDB) plus S3 object storage. Per-tenant data segregation explicitly implemented. Backup policy exists with retention periods. No caching layer named. No data warehouse. Backup frequency not specified as daily.

security_posture.rationale: Annual external pen test, monthly vuln scans, IDS/IPS via AWS, firewalls, AV/EDR on all endpoints and production servers, MFA for admin/prod access, quarterly access reviews, 1-day access revocation SLA. No WAF explicitly named. No SIEM named.

devops_maturity.rationale: Branch protection with merge approval required, separate environments, emergency change process, monthly patch management. IaC-style config management referenced but no named tool. CI/CD pipeline tool not named. Source control tool not named.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented, annual review and testing conducted across multiple loss scenarios. Multi-AZ failover via AWS. No multi-region DR. No RTO/RPO disclosed. Backup retention periods defined but daily backup frequency not confirmed.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Heavy AWS concentration (compute, database, AI via Bedrock, networking). Vercel for frontend. AWS Bedrock is a critical dependency for core AI functionality — single vendor risk if Bedrock has issues. Only 3 named vendors total.

overall.rationale: Solid early-stage SOC 2 Type 1 posture. Modern tech stack (Next.js, App Runner, RDS, DynamoDB, Bedrock), multi-tenant segregation, good access controls and annual pen testing. Key gaps: single region, unnamed CI/CD tooling, heavy Bedrock/AWS concentration, no RTO/RPO disclosure, no WAF or SIEM named.

red_flags: Single AWS region (us-east-2) only — no multi-region disaster recovery
Heavy dependency on AWS Bedrock for core AI functionality — no fallback LLM provider mentioned
No RTO or RPO disclosed despite BCDR policy existing
No WAF explicitly mentioned in controls
yellow_flags: CI/CD tool not named — pipeline maturity unclear
Source control system not named (only referenced as 'source code management tool')
IaC tool not specifically named — 'configuration management tool' referenced generically
Backup frequency not confirmed as daily — only 'suitable backups are taken'
No SIEM tool named — log aggregation approach unclear
Processing Integrity and Privacy criteria excluded — may be relevant for enterprise customers uploading sensitive corporate data
green_flags: Network diagram explicitly shows architecture: Vercel + App Runner + VPC with private subnets + RDS + DynamoDB + S3 + Bedrock
Multi-tenant data segregation with unique client identifier on all data — explicitly described
MFA enforced for admin and production access, and for source code tool
Quarterly access reviews for all accounts including privileged users
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party
Branch protection with mandatory approval for all production merges
BCDR tested annually across multiple loss scenarios
AWS KMS for encryption at rest
Multi-AZ failover via AWS for production servers
Passwordless authentication for end-users (modern auth approach)
key_observations: AWS Bedrock is the AI backbone — this is a key architectural dependency that reviewers should probe for SLA, data residency, and model versioning commitments

The use of Vercel for the frontend (www + application servers) alongside AWS for backend creates a split-CDN/compute architecture — Vercel outages could impact user-facing availability independently of AWS health
The product ingests sensitive enterprise corporate data (strategy memos, product requirements, market analysis) — the per-tenant segregation claim is critical and should be validated technically

Trust criteria listed in auditor scope includes all 5 criteria (Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, Privacy) but the company itself determined Processing Integrity and Privacy are not relevant — this is a mismatch worth flagging for enterprise buyers
App Runner as compute is a managed container service without Kubernetes complexity — lower DevOps overhead but less control over scaling and isolation
